docs.title: PostgreSQL Partition Manager
docs.text: PostgreSQL Partition Manager (PPM) is an opinionated tool designed to streamline the management of PostgreSQL partitions. It operates on PostgreSQL's declarative partitioning and does not require any specific PostgreSQL extensions.

docs.title: Why PPM?
docs.text: The key objective of PPM is to simplify the use of PostgreSQL partitions for developers. By providing a secure, non-blocking, and intuitive tool for partition management, developers can manage table schema and indexes using existing tools and ORMs, and leverage PPM to implement date-based and UUIDv7-based partitioning for scaling PostgreSQL systems.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Automatic provisioning — Create upcoming partitions ahead of time
  • Cleanup management — Delete or detach outdated partitions
  • Configuration checking — Verify partitions match expected configuration
  • Multiple partition intervals — Support for daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, and yearly partitioning
  • Flexible partition keys — Support for date, timestamp, timestamptz, and uuid column types

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Head over to the Getting Started guide to set up PPM in minutes, or explore the Installation page for all available installation methods.

docs.title: postgresql-partition-manager
docs.text:
Simplified PostgreSQL partitioning management

Usage:

postgresql-partition-manager

Flags:

Flag Shorthand Default Description --config -c "" config file (default is $HOME/postgresql-partition-manager.yaml) --connection-url -u "" Database connection string --debug -d false Enable debug mode --lock-timeout 100 Set lock_timeout (ms) --log-format -l json Log format (text or json) --statement-timeout 3000 Set statement_timeout (ms)

docs.title: postgresql-partition-manager run
docs.text:
Perform partition operations

Usage:

postgresql-partition-manager run

Inherited Flags:

Flag Shorthand Default Description --config -c "" config file (default is $HOME/postgresql-partition-manager.yaml) --connection-url -u "" Database connection string --debug -d false Enable debug mode --lock-timeout 100 Set lock_timeout (ms) --log-format -l json Log format (text or json) --statement-timeout 3000 Set statement_timeout (ms)

Flag Shorthand Default Description --config -c "" config file (default is $HOME/postgresql-partition-manager.yaml) --connection-url -u "" Database connection string --debug -d false Enable debug mode --lock-timeout 100 Set lock_timeout (ms) --log-format -l json Log format (text or json) --statement-timeout 3000 Set statement_timeout (ms)

Flag Shorthand Default Description --config -c "" config file (default is $HOME/postgresql-partition-manager.yaml) --connection-url -u "" Database connection string --debug -d false Enable debug mode --lock-timeout 100 Set lock_timeout (ms) --log-format -l json Log format (text or json) --statement-timeout 3000 Set statement_timeout (ms)

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: PPM can be configured through a YAML configuration file and environment variables. This page describes all available configuration options and how they interact.

docs.title: Configuration File
docs.text: By default, PPM looks for a postgresql-partition-manager.yaml file in the current directory or your home directory. You can specify a custom path with the --config flag.

docs.title: Full Configuration Example
docs.text:
# Enable debug mode
debug: false

# Log format (text or json)
log-format: json

# PostgreSQL connection URL
connection-url: postgres://username:password@localhost:5432/mydb

# Maximum allowed duration of any wait for a lock (milliseconds)
# Prevents infinite locks when long-running transactions occur
lock-timeout: 300

# Maximum allowed duration of any statement (milliseconds)
statement-timeout: 3000

# Partitions definition
partitions:
  my_logs:
    schema: public
    table: logs
    partitionKey: created_at
    interval: daily
    retention: 30
    preProvisioned: 7
    cleanupPolicy: drop

docs.title: Global Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description Default connection-url PostgreSQL connection URL debug Enable debug mode false log-format Log format (text or json) json lock-timeout Maximum allowed duration of any wait for a lock (ms) 300 statement-timeout Maximum allowed duration of any statement (ms) 3000 partitions Map of partition configurations

docs.title: Partition Parameters
docs.text:
Each entry in the partitions map defines a managed partition:

Parameter Description Default schema PostgreSQL schema containing the table table Table to be partitioned partitionKey Column used for partitioning interval Partitioning interval (daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, or yearly) preProvisioned Number of partitions to create in advance retention Number of partitions to retain cleanupPolicy Cleanup behavior: drop (detach and drop) or detach (detach only)

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
All configuration parameters can be overridden using environment variables. The prefix is POSTGRESQL_PARTITION_MANAGER_ followed by the uppercase parameter name with hyphens replaced by underscores.

Configuration Key Environment Variable debug POSTGRESQL_PARTITION_MANAGER_DEBUG log-format POSTGRESQL_PARTITION_MANAGER_LOG_FORMAT connection-url POSTGRESQL_PARTITION_MANAGER_CONNECTION_URL lock-timeout POSTGRESQL_PARTITION_MANAGER_LOCK_TIMEOUT statement-timeout POSTGRESQL_PARTITION_MANAGER_STATEMENT_TIMEOUT

docs.title: Partition Naming
docs.text:
Partition names are automatically generated based on the interval:

Interval Pattern Example daily <table>_<YYYY>_<DD>_<MM> logs_2024_06_25 weekly <table>_w<ISO week> logs_2024_w26 monthly <table>_<YYYY>_<MM> logs_2024_06 quarterly <table>_<YYYY>_q<quarter> logs_2024_q1 yearly <table>_<YYYY> logs_2024

Partition names are not configurable.

docs.title: Configuration Precedence
docs.text:
Configuration values are resolved in the following order (highest priority first):

  1. CLI flags (--connection-url, --debug, etc.)
  2. Environment variables (POSTGRESQL_PARTITION_MANAGER_*)
  3. Configuration file (postgresql-partition-manager.yaml)
  4. Default values

docs.title: Supported Column Types
docs.text:
The partition key must be a column of one of the following types:

  • date
  • timestamp
  • timestamptz
  • uuid (UUIDv7)

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: This page provides real-world usage examples for common PPM deployment scenarios. Each example includes a complete configuration and the commands to run.

docs.title: Daily Log Table Partitioning
docs.text:
Partition a high-volume logs table by day, keeping 30 days of data and pre-provisioning 7 days ahead:

connection-url: postgres://app_user:password@db.example.com:5432/production

partitions:
  application_logs:
    schema: public
    table: logs
    partitionKey: created_at
    interval: daily
    retention: 30
    preProvisioned: 7
    cleanupPolicy: drop

postgresql-partition-manager run all

docs.title: Monthly Events with Detach-Only Cleanup
docs.text:
For audit tables where data must be retained but detached from the active table for performance:

connection-url: postgres://audit_user@db.example.com/audit_db

partitions:
  audit_events:
    schema: audit
    table: events
    partitionKey: event_date
    interval: monthly
    retention: 12
    preProvisioned: 3
    cleanupPolicy: detach

docs.title: UUIDv7-Based Partitioning
docs.text:
Partition a table using UUIDv7 primary keys, which encode a timestamp:

connection-url: postgres://app@db.example.com/myapp

partitions:
  orders:
    schema: public
    table: orders
    partitionKey: id
    interval: monthly
    retention: 6
    preProvisioned: 2
    cleanupPolicy: drop

docs.title: Multiple Tables with Different Intervals
docs.text:
Manage several tables with varying partition strategies in a single configuration:

connection-url: postgres://admin@db.example.com/production

partitions:
  high_volume_logs:
    schema: public
    table: request_logs
    partitionKey: created_at
    interval: daily
    retention: 14
    preProvisioned: 7
    cleanupPolicy: drop

  session_data:
    schema: public
    table: sessions
    partitionKey: started_at
    interval: weekly
    retention: 12
    preProvisioned: 4
    cleanupPolicy: drop

  analytics:
    schema: reporting
    table: metrics
    partitionKey: recorded_at
    interval: monthly
    retention: 24
    preProvisioned: 3
    cleanupPolicy: detach

postgresql-partition-manager run all

docs.title: Docker Deployment
docs.text:
Run PPM as a one-shot container with an external configuration file:

docker run \
  -v ./postgresql-partition-manager.yaml:/app/postgresql-partition-manager.yaml \
  public.ecr.aws/qonto/postgresql-partition-manager:latest \
  run all

docs.title: Testing with Work Date Override
docs.text:
Test how PPM would behave on a specific date without waiting:

PPM_WORK_DATE=2024-12-31 postgresql-partition-manager run all --debug --log-format text

docs.title: Validate Configuration in CI
docs.text:
Add a validation step to your CI pipeline:

postgresql-partition-manager validate --config configs/ppm-production.yaml

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This guide walks you through installing and running PPM for the first time. By the end, you will have PPM managing partitions on a PostgreSQL database.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• PostgreSQL 14 or higher
  • A table using declarative RANGE partitioning
  • A partition key column of type date, timestamp, timestamptz, or uuid

docs.title: 1. Install PPM
docs.text:
Download the latest binary for your platform:

# Using Go install
go install github.com/qonto/postgresql-partition-manager@latest

See Installation for other methods (Docker, Helm, Debian package).

docs.title: 2. Create a Sample Partitioned Table
docs.text:
Create a logs table partitioned by created_at:

CREATE TABLE public.logs (
    id BIGSERIAL,
    message TEXT,
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
) PARTITION BY RANGE (created_at);

docs.title: 3. Create a Configuration File
docs.text:
Create a postgresql-partition-manager.yaml file:

debug: false
log-format: json
connection-url: postgres://username:password@localhost/mydb

partitions:
  my_logs:
    schema: public
    table: logs
    partitionKey: created_at
    interval: monthly
    retention: 12
    preProvisioned: 3
    cleanupPolicy: drop

docs.title: 4. Validate Your Configuration
docs.text: postgresql-partition-manager validate

docs.title: 5. Run Partition Management
docs.text:
Execute all operations in once (partition provisioning, partition cleanup (detach or drop), and check):

postgresql-partition-manager run all

docs.title: 6. Verify Created Partitions
docs.text:
Check the partitions created by PPM:

psql -c '\d+ public.logs'

Expected output:

                                          Partitioned table "public.logs"
   Column   |           Type           | Collation | Nullable |             Default              | Storage  | Compression | Stats target | Description
------------+--------------------------+-----------+----------+----------------------------------+----------+-------------+--------------+-------------
 id         | bigint                   |           | not null | nextval('logs_id_seq'::regclass) | plain    |             |              |
 message    | text                     |           |          |                                  | extended |             |              |
 created_at | timestamp with time zone |           | not null | now()                            | plain    |             |              |
Partition key: RANGE (created_at)
Not-null constraints:
    "logs_id_not_null" NOT NULL "id"
    "logs_created_at_not_null" NOT NULL "created_at"
Partitions: logs_2025_06 FOR VALUES FROM ('2025-06-01 00:00:00+00') TO ('2025-07-01 00:00:00+00'),
            logs_2025_07 FOR VALUES FROM ('2025-07-01 00:00:00+00') TO ('2025-08-01 00:00:00+00'),
            logs_2025_08 FOR VALUES FROM ('2025-08-01 00:00:00+00') TO ('2025-09-01 00:00:00+00'),
            [...]

docs.title: What Happens Next?
docs.text:
PPM will:

  1. Provision future partitions based on the preProvisioned setting
  2. Cleanup outdated partitions beyond the retention period
  3. Check that existing partitions match the expected configuration

docs.title: Recommended Setup
docs.text: We recommend running postgresql-partition-manager run all daily via a CRON job or Kubernetes CronJob, with a minimum of 3 pre-provisioned partitions (7 for daily partitioning). Set up alerts on non-zero exit codes to detect partition issues early.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Installation — All installation methods
  • Configuration — Full configuration reference
  • Usage — Detailed CLI usage patterns
  • Examples — Real-world usage scenarios

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: PPM is available as a Go binary, Docker image, Debian package, and Helm chart for Kubernetes deployments. Choose the method that best fits your infrastructure.

docs.title: Go Install
docs.text:
Install the latest version directly using Go:

go install github.com/qonto/postgresql-partition-manager@latest

docs.title: Authenticate to AWS ECR Public
docs.text:
Before pulling the image, authenticate to the AWS ECR Public registry:

aws ecr-public get-login-password --region us-east-1 | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin public.ecr.aws

docs.title: Pull the Image
docs.text: docker pull public.ecr.aws/qonto/postgresql-partition-manager:latest

docs.title: Generate a Configuration File
docs.text: Create a postgresql-partition-manager.yaml file in your working directory. See Configuration for the full reference.

docs.title: Run with a Configuration File
docs.text:
Mount your configuration file and run PPM:

docker run -v ./postgresql-partition-manager.yaml:/app/postgresql-partition-manager.yaml \
  public.ecr.aws/qonto/postgresql-partition-manager:latest

docs.title: Download and Install
docs.text:
POSTGRESQL_PARTITION_MANAGER_VERSION=0.1.0  # Replace with latest version

PACKAGE_NAME=postgresql-partition-manager_${POSTGRESQL_PARTITION_MANAGER_VERSION}_$(uname -m).deb
wget https://github.com/qonto/postgresql-partition-manager/releases/download/${POSTGRESQL_PARTITION_MANAGER_VERSION}/${PACKAGE_NAME}
dpkg -i ${PACKAGE_NAME}

docs.title: Configure
docs.text:
Copy the sample configuration file and customize it:

cp /usr/share/postgresql-partition-manager/postgresql-partition-manager.yaml.sample postgresql-partition-manager.yaml
vim postgresql-partition-manager.yaml

docs.title: 1. Create a Kubernetes Secret
docs.text:
Store your PostgreSQL password in a Kubernetes secret:

kubectl create secret generic postgresql-credentials \
  --from-literal=password=replace_with_your_postgresql_password

For production, consider using the Kubernetes Secrets Store CSI driver.

docs.title: 2. Create a Values File
docs.text:
Copy and edit the Helm charts values:

cat > values.yaml << EOF
cronjob:
  postgresqlPasswordSecret:
    ref: postgresql-credentials
    key: password

configuration:
  debug: false
  connection-url: postgres://my_username@postgres/my_app

  partitions:
    my_partition:
      schema: public
      table: logs
      partitionKey: created_at
      interval: daily
      retention: 30
      preProvisioned: 7
      cleanupPolicy: drop
EOF

vim values.yaml

PPM configuration is located under configuration key. See Configuration for the full reference.

docs.title: 3. Deploy the Chart
docs.text:
POSTGRESQL_PARTITION_MANAGER_VERSION=0.1.0  # Replace with latest version
KUBERNETES_NAMESPACE=default # Replace with your namespace
HELM_RELEASE_NAME=main # Replace with an helm release

helm upgrade \
  ${HELM_RELEASE_NAME} \
  oci://public.ecr.aws/qonto/postgresql-partition-manager-chart \
  --version ${POSTGRESQL_PARTITION_MANAGER_VERSION} \
  --install \
  --namespace ${KUBERNETES_NAMESPACE} \
  --values values.yaml

docs.title: 4. Verify Deployment
docs.text:
Trigger a manual job to verify everything works:

MANUAL_JOB_NAME=ppm-manually-triggered

kubectl create job \
  --namespace ${KUBERNETES_NAMESPACE} \
  --from=cronjob/${HELM_RELEASE_NAME}-postgresql-partition-manager-chart \
  ${MANUAL_JOB_NAME}

kubectl logs --namespace ${KUBERNETES_NAMESPACE} --selector=job-name=${MANUAL_JOB_NAME}

Clean up the manual job:

kubectl delete job --namespace ${KUBERNETES_NAMESPACE} ${MANUAL_JOB_NAME}

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: This page covers common issues encountered when using PPM and how to resolve them.

docs.title: Configuration File Not Found
docs.text:
Symptom: PPM starts without applying your configuration.

Solution: Ensure the configuration file is named postgresql-partition-manager.yaml and located in either the current directory or your home directory. Alternatively, specify the path explicitly:

postgresql-partition-manager run all --config /path/to/postgresql-partition-manager.yaml

docs.title: Database Connection Refused
docs.text:
Symptom: Exit code 3 with a "Could not connect to database" error.

Solution: Verify your connection URL is correct and the database is reachable:

# Test connection with psql
psql "postgres://user:password@host:5432/dbname"

Check that:

  • The hostname and port are correct
  • The database user has appropriate permissions
  • Network/firewall rules allow the connection
  • SSL requirements are met (add ?sslmode=require if needed)

docs.title: Lock Timeout Exceeded
docs.text:
Symptom: Operations fail due to lock timeout.

Solution: This means another transaction is holding a lock on the partition table. Increase the lock timeout or investigate the blocking transaction:

postgresql-partition-manager run all --lock-timeout 1000

To identify blocking queries in PostgreSQL:

SELECT pid, state, query, wait_event_type
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE wait_event_type = 'Lock';

docs.title: Statement Timeout Exceeded
docs.text:
Symptom: Operations fail with a statement timeout error.

Solution: For large tables with many partitions, the default timeout may be insufficient. Increase it:

postgresql-partition-manager run all --statement-timeout 10000

docs.title: Invalid Configuration (Exit Code 1)
docs.text:
Symptom: PPM exits immediately with code 1.

Solution: Run the validate command to get detailed error information:

postgresql-partition-manager validate --debug --log-format text

Common configuration errors:

  • Missing required fields (schema, table, partitionKey, interval, retention, preProvisioned, cleanupPolicy)
  • Invalid interval value (must be daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, or yearly)
  • Invalid cleanupPolicy value (must be drop or detach)

docs.title: Partition Check Failed (Exit Code 5)
docs.text:
Symptom: run check or run all exits with code 5.

Cause: Existing partitions don't match the expected configuration. This can happen when:

  • Partitions were manually created with different boundaries
  • The interval was changed without proper migration
  • Gaps exist between partitions

Solution: Investigate with debug logging enabled:

postgresql-partition-manager run check --debug --log-format text

Review the output to identify which partitions are misaligned and correct them manually or adjust your configuration.

docs.title: Partition Provisioning Failed (Exit Code 4)
docs.text:
Symptom: run provisioning exits with code 4.

Possible causes:

  • Insufficient permissions to create partitions
  • Conflicting partition ranges already exist
  • Table does not exist or is not partitioned

Solution: Ensure the database user has CREATE permission on the schema, and verify the parent table is set up with declarative RANGE partitioning.

docs.title: Invalid Work Date (Exit Code 7)
docs.text:
Symptom: Exit code 7 when using PPM_WORK_DATE.

Solution: Ensure the date format is YYYY-MM-DD:

# Correct
PPM_WORK_DATE=2024-06-15 postgresql-partition-manager run all

# Wrong
PPM_WORK_DATE=15-06-2024 postgresql-partition-manager run all

docs.title: Debug Mode
docs.text:
Enable debug mode for verbose logging to diagnose issues:

postgresql-partition-manager run all --debug --log-format text

This outputs detailed information about each operation, including SQL queries executed and partition discovery results.

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
If you encounter an issue not covered here:

  1. Check the GitHub Issues for known problems
  2. Open a new issue with debug logs and your configuration (redact credentials)

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: PPM provides a command-line interface built on Cobra for managing PostgreSQL partitions. This page covers all available commands, their options, and common usage patterns.

docs.title: Global Flags
docs.text:
These flags are available on all commands:

Flag Short Default Description --config -c Config file path (default: $HOME/postgresql-partition-manager.yaml) --debug -d false Enable debug mode --log-format -l json Log format (text or json) --connection-url -u Database connection string --lock-timeout 100 Set lock_timeout in milliseconds --statement-timeout 3000 Set statement_timeout in milliseconds

docs.title: Commands
docs.text: See CLI reference for the full reference.

docs.title: Daily Scheduled Execution
docs.text:
Run all partition operations daily with a custom config:

postgresql-partition-manager run all --config /etc/ppm/postgresql-partition-manager.yaml

docs.title: Validate Before Deploy
docs.text:
Check configuration validity in CI before deploying a new config:

postgresql-partition-manager validate --config postgresql-partition-manager.yaml

docs.title: Debug Mode
docs.text:
Enable verbose logging for troubleshooting:

postgresql-partition-manager run all --debug --log-format text

docs.title: Custom Timeouts
docs.text:
Increase timeouts for large databases:

postgresql-partition-manager run all --lock-timeout 500 --statement-timeout 10000

docs.title: Override Connection URL
docs.text:
Pass the connection URL via flag or environment variable:

# Via flag
postgresql-partition-manager run all --connection-url "postgres://user:pass@host/db"

# Via environment variable
export POSTGRESQL_PARTITION_MANAGER_CONNECTION_URL="postgres://user:pass@host/db"
postgresql-partition-manager run all

docs.title: Work Date Override
docs.text:
By default, provisioning and cleanup evaluate what to do at the current date. For testing purposes, a different date can be set through the environment variable PPM_WORK_DATE:

PPM_WORK_DATE=2024-06-15 postgresql-partition-manager run all

The date format is YYYY-MM-DD.

docs.title: Exit Codes
docs.text:
PPM uses specific exit codes to indicate the type of failure:

Code Meaning 0 Success 1 Invalid configuration 2 Internal error 3 Database connection error 4 Partition provisioning failed 5 Partition check failed 6 Partition cleanup failed 7 Invalid work date

Monitor these exit codes in your alerting system to detect partition issues early.
